name: Chantada - tour around the Miño River and Os Peares Dam Reservoir
name_original: Chantada – vuelta por río Miño y Presa de Embalse os Peares
description: Cycle a difficult 43.6-mile route around the Miño River and Os Peares Dam Reservoir, featuring vineyards and river views.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Chantada traverse a landscape characterized by the dramatic Miño Canyon, terraced vineyards, and the elevated terrain of Monte Faro. The region features a blend of riverside paths along the Miño and Asma rivers, alongside more challenging routes through mountainous areas. This diverse topography offers varied cycling experiences, from gentle riverside promenades to climbs through the Galician countryside. Best touring cycling routes around Chantada The most popular touring cycling route is Chantada - tour around the Miño River and Os Peares Dam Reservoir, a 43.6 miles (70.1 km) trail that takes 5 hours 10 minutes to complete. This route explores the Miño River and one of Galicia's oldest reservoirs. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Chantada - tour around Roderio – Special Conservation Area Monte Faro, a difficult 30.2 miles (48.5 km) path. This trail offers elevated views from the Monte Faro protected area. Local touring cyclists also love the Taboada – tour around the Miño and Chantada rivers, a 37.3 miles (60.0 km) trail leading through river valleys, often completed in about 4 hours 6 minutes. Touring cycling around Chantada is defined by river canyons, terraced vineyards, and elevated hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more challenging climbs. The routes in Chantada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 65 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Chantada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Chantada?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes available around Chantada, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. The komoot community has explored these routes extensively, with over 65 touring cyclists using komoot to discover the area.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Chantada?
A: The terrain around Chantada is quite varied, characterized by the dramatic Miño Canyon, terraced vineyards, and the elevated landscapes of Monte Faro. You'll find a blend of riverside paths along the Miño and Asma rivers, alongside more challenging routes through mountainous areas. Most routes are classified as moderate to difficult, with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Chantada?
A: While many routes in Chantada are moderate to difficult due to the hilly terrain, there is at least one easy touring cycling route available. For families, it's advisable to look for routes that stick to the flatter riverside promenades, such as those along the Asma River, which offer more gentle cycling experiences.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while cycling in Chantada?
A: Cycling in Chantada offers access to numerous natural and historical attractions. You can visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5016863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, one of Galicia's highest, or explore the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6496544" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribeira Sacra River Valley&lt;/a&gt; with its terraced vineyards. Routes also pass by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6880090" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belesar Bridge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6570496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Outdoor Altar of Nosa Señora do Faro&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking views from Monte Faro.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Chantada region?
A: Yes, there are several circular touring cycling routes available. For instance, the popular route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017341434/chantada-tour-around-the-mino-river-and-os-peares-dam-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chantada - tour around the Miño River and Os Peares Dam Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is a circular tour that explores the Miño River and one of Galicia's oldest reservoirs. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017432730/chantada-tour-around-roderio-special-conservation-area-monte-faro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chantada - tour around Roderio – Special Conservation Area Monte Faro&lt;/a&gt;, which offers elevated views in a loop.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Chantada?
A: Chantada's climate makes spring and autumn ideal for touring cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be warm, especially in the river valleys, while winters are generally mild but can bring rain. Checking local weather forecasts before your trip is always recommended.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Chantada?
A: Parking is generally available in and around Chantada town center, as well as in smaller villages that serve as starting points for routes. Many routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31439814" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lego Galician Folk Mural – Chantada Town Center loop&lt;/a&gt;, begin directly from the town, making parking accessible.

Q: Are there options for public transport to access touring cycling routes in Chantada?
A: Public transport options to access touring cycling routes in Chantada are limited, as is common in rural Galician regions. It's often most convenient to travel to Chantada by car and then use local parking. Some bus services connect Chantada to larger towns, but their schedules and bike-carrying capacity should be verified in advance.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Chantada?
A: Experienced cyclists looking for a challenge will find several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017432730/chantada-tour-around-roderio-special-conservation-area-monte-faro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chantada - tour around Roderio – Special Conservation Area Monte Faro&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 30.2-mile path with significant elevation, offering elevated views. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018478404/taboada-tour-around-the-mino-and-chantada-rivers" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Taboada – tour around the Miño and Chantada rivers&lt;/a&gt;, a 37.3-mile route through river valleys with considerable climbs.

Q: What do other cyclists say about the touring routes in Chantada?
A: The touring cycling routes in Chantada are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning natural beauty, particularly the river canyons and terraced vineyards, and the varied terrain that offers both scenic beauty and a good workout.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through charming villages or historical sites?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Chantada weave through picturesque villages and past historical sites. The region is rich in Romanesque architecture, and you can find routes that allow you to explore ancient churches and monasteries. The village of Belesar, for example, known for its traditional architecture and significant bridge, is often part of scenic river routes along the Miño.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the cycling routes?
A: Chantada and the surrounding villages offer various options for food and accommodation. You'll find local cafes, restaurants, and rural guesthouses (casas rurales) that cater to visitors. The region's wine tourism also means opportunities to visit wineries and taste local produce, often with accompanying eateries.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Miño Canyon?
A: Yes, the Miño Canyon is a defining feature of the Chantada region, and many touring cycling routes offer spectacular views of its dramatic scenery. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017341434/chantada-tour-around-the-mino-river-and-os-peares-dam-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chantada - tour around the Miño River and Os Peares Dam Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; route, for instance, directly explores the Miño River, providing ample opportunities to take in the canyon's beauty and the terraced vineyards.

Q: Are there any routes that include a waterfall?
A: Yes, you can incorporate a visit to the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5016863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; into your cycling itinerary. This natural wonder, one of Galicia's highest, is located within the Ribeira Sacra and can be accessed via routes that pass through the area, offering a rewarding stop during your tour.
tips: Chantada is a regional commercial city located on the left bank of the Asma River, halfway along the commercial route between Ourense and Lugo.
Natural park with trails along the Asma River as it passes through Chantada. Native landscape surrounded by river water and trees.
Tall waterfall through the jagged rocky landscape carved by nature through the centuries in the Mino river basin.
Hydroelectric dam on the river Miño upstream before the mighty river meets the river Sil at os Peares. Crossing the dam gives you an incredible view of the power of the water.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and is generally considered demanding for touring cyclists. It features an irregular orography with consistent uphill stretches, particularly for about 34 kilometers from Chantada towards Os Peares. While the technical difficulty might be low on some sections, the continuous ascent requires good fitness and is best suited for experienced touring cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse. You can expect a mix of quiet asphalted tracks and natural road surfaces, including paths and trails. Roughly half of the route, especially the ascending sections towards Os Peares, is on asphalted tracks, while the other half is on natural road surfaces, which may include gravel. The region's 'irregular orography' means varied surfaces and gradients.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is typically milder, and the natural scenery, especially the vineyards of Ribeira Sacra, is particularly beautiful. Summer can be very hot, and winter may bring colder temperatures and more unpredictable weather.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the town of Chantada, which serves as the starting point for this route. Look for public parking areas within or near the Chantada Town Center. Specific parking details may vary, so it's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers numerous scenic and cultural highlights. You'll pass through the Chantada Town Center and along the Río Asma Riverside Walk. Key natural features include the Fondós Waterfall and the dramatic confluence of the Sil and Miño rivers near the Os Peares Dam. The entire route is immersed in the Ribeira Sacra region, famous for its 'heroic viticulture' and Romanesque architecture, offering panoramic views of river canyons and terraced vineyards.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through natural areas, specific regulations for dogs on bicycles may vary. Generally, dogs are allowed on public paths, but it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially in populated areas, near livestock, or on shared paths. Always ensure your dog is comfortable with the distance and terrain, and carry enough water for them.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need specific permits or to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. The trail is located within the Reserva da Biosfera Ribeira Sacra e Serras do Oribio e Courel, but it consists of public roads and paths. However, always respect private property, follow local regulations, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route may overlap with other known paths. Notably, about 15% of the route follows parts of the Camiño de Inverno. You might also encounter very small segments of the Paseo fluvial do Río Asma and Paseo do Sangoñedo.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or refill water along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, you'll find options for food and water, particularly in the larger towns like Chantada at the start. As you cycle through smaller villages and near Os Peares, there may be cafes or small shops. However, due to the route's length and demanding nature, it's highly recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer, more remote sections.

faqs.question: What is a unique feature or insider tip for this route?
faqs.answer: A unique feature of this route is its immersion in the Ribeira Sacra region, renowned for its 'heroic viticulture.' You'll cycle past vineyards clinging to incredibly steep riverbanks. An insider tip for tackling the demanding ascent towards Os Peares is to start early in the day to avoid the midday heat, especially in warmer months, and to allow ample time to appreciate the stunning views without feeling rushed.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' classification, significant elevation gain (1525m up), and mixed terrain including natural road surfaces, this route is generally not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It requires good fitness and experience with touring cycling. Shorter, flatter sections might be suitable for families, but the full route is challenging.
